messages.content:
1.  First of all, I'm a geek. I'm an organic food eating, carbon footprint minimizing robotic surgery geek. And I really want to build green. But I'm very suspicious of all of these well meaning articles, people long on moral authority and short on data telling me how to do these kinds of things. And so I have to figure this out for myself. For example is this. I have dropped a blob of organic yogurt from happy self actualized local cows on my counter top and I grab a paper towel and I want to wipe it up. But can I use a paper towel? The answer to this can be found in embodied energy. This is the amount of energy that goes into any paper tell or embodied water. And every time I use a paper tell, I'm using this much virtual energy and water by put it up, throw it away. Now if I compare that to a cotton tell that I can use 1,000 times, I don't have a whole lot of embodied energy until I wash that yogurt de tell. This is now operating energy. So if I throw my tell in the washing machine, I've now put energy and water back into that tell unless I use a front loading high efficiency washing machine and then it looks a little bit better but. About a recycled paper tell that comes in this little hat sheets. Well, a nail paper tell looks better. The paper tells, let's go to a sponge. I wipe it with a sponge and I put it under the running water and I have a lot less energy and a lot more water, unless you like me, and you leave the handle in the position of a hot even when you turn it on, and then you start to use more energy. Or worse, you let it run until it's warm to rinse after your tell. And now I'll betr off. So what this says is that sometimes the things that you least expect, the position in which you put the handle, have a bigger effect than any of those other things that you were trying to optimize. Now imagine someone is twisted as me trying to build a house. So That's what my husband and I are doing right now. And so we wanted to know how green could we be? And there's a thousand and one articles out there telling us how to make all these green tradeoffs and they are just a suspect and telling us to optimize these little things around the edges and missing the elephant in the living room. Now the average house has about 300 megawatt hours of embodied energy in it. This is the energy it takes to make it of millions of paper towels. We wanted to know how much better we could do. And so like many people, we start with a house on a lot. And I'm going to show you typical instruction on the top and what we're doing on the bottom. So first we demolish it. Take some energy. But if you deconstruct it, you take it all apart, you use the bits, you can get some of that energy back. We then dug a big hole to put in a rainwater catch tank to take our yard water independent. And then we poured a big foundation for passive solar. And you can reduce the embodied energy by about 25% by using high fly ash concrete. We then put in framing. And so this is framing lumber, composite materials. And it's kind of hard to get the embodied energy out of that, but it can be a sustainable resource if you use F certified lumber.
    
2.    
    
3.  We then go on to the first thing that was very surprising. If we put aluminum windows in this house, we would double the energy use right there. Now PVC is a little bit better, but still not as good as the wood that we chose. We then put in plumbing, electrical and HVAC and insulate. Now it spray foam is an excellent insulator. It fills in all of the cracks, but it is pretty high body. Blue jeans is a much lower energy alternative to that. We also used straw bale in fill for our library, which has zero embodied energy. When you comes time to sheet rock, if you use echo rock, it's about a quarter of the embodied energy of standard sheet rock. And then you get to finish. It's the subjects of all of those Go green articles. And on the of a house, they almost make no difference at all. And yet all the press is focused on that except for flooring. If you put carpeting in your house, it's about a tenth of the embodied energy of the entire house unless you use concrete or wood for a much lower embodied energy. So now we add in the final construction energy, we add it all up and we built a house for less than half of the typical embodied energy for building a house like this. Before we patter ourselves to much on the back. We have poured 151 megawatt hours of energy into constructing this house when there was a house there before. And so the question is how could we make that back? And so if I run my new energy efficient house forward compared to the old non energy efficient house, we make it back in about six years. Now I probably would have upgrading the old house to be more energy efficient. And if that case, it would take me more about 20 years to break even. If I now if I hadn't paid attention to embodied energy, it would have taken us over 50 years to break even compared to the upgraded house. So what is this mean? On the scale of my portion of the house, this is equivalent to about as much as I drive in a year. It's about five times as much as if I went entirely vegetarian. But my elephant in the living room flies.
    
  
Actually, I need to walk home from Ted, but all the calculations for embodied energy are on the block. And remember, it's sometimes the things that you are not expecting to be the biggest to changes that are. Thank you.
  
  
1.  (1)
    
    1. Low embodied energy: ______________________________________________
    
    多选题(2 分)
    
    *     
        
    
    1.  A.
        
        spray foam
        
    2.  B.
        
        wood
        
    3.  C.
        
         carpeting the floor
        
    4.  D.
        
        fly ash concrete
        
    5.  E.
        
        sprayed-in cellulose
        
    6.  F.
        
         aluminum window
        
    7.  G.
        
        straw bale
        
2.  (2)
    
    High embodied energy: _____________________
    
    多选题(2 分)
    
    *     
        
    
    1.  A.
        
         straw bale
        
    2.  B.
        
         aluminum window
        
    3.  C.
        
        sprayed-in cellulose 
        
    4.  D.
        
         spray foam
        
    5.  E.
        
        fly ash concrete
        
    6.  F.
        
        wood
        
    7.  G.
        
        carpeting the floor

From the talk:

- **Spray foam** is described as “pretty high embodied energy.”
- **Aluminum windows** would “double the energy use right there” (very high embodied energy).
- **Carpeting** is “about a tenth of the embodied energy of the entire house” (high).
- **Fly ash concrete** is used to *reduce* embodied energy (lower than standard concrete).
- **Wood windows** are better than PVC/aluminum (lower).
- **Straw bale** infill is said to have “zero embodied energy” (very low).
- **Sprayed-in cellulose** isn’t explicitly discussed in the provided excerpt, but it’s generally treated as a low-embodied-energy insulation (recycled paper). Given the quiz context, it belongs with the low group.

### (1) Low embodied energy:
**B, D, E, G**  
- **B. wood**  
- **D. fly ash concrete**  
- **E. sprayed-in cellulose**  
- **G. straw bale**

### (2) High embodied energy:
**B, D, G**  
- **B. aluminum window**  
- **D. spray foam**  
- **G. carpeting the floor**
